document_summary.primary_finding: The Conkling Letter deploys four clusters in a single document — the most since the Gettysburg Address (all six). Three cluster_01 instances span the letter: the nation's life (p07), the proclamation's life/death legal status (p11), and the republic's vital principle (p14). The cluster_02 instance (p08_s28) is the most explicit naming of the Constitution-as-covenant in the corpus: 'the bond of service--the United States Constitution.' Lincoln names the bond and its source in the same breath. The cluster_03 instance (p15_s03) is the war-as-proof formula in its most accessible form: 'proved that...there can be no successful appeal from the ballot to the bullet.' The cluster_06 closing (p16_s03) — 'a just God, in his own good time, will give us the rightful result' — frames God as the manager of historical timing, a Providence frame more active than the Springfield Farewell but less punitive than the Second Inaugural. Clusters absent: cluster_04 (no birth/creation) and cluster_05 (no founding fathers). The letter is most significant for what it says about Black soldiers: p11_s08 explicitly attributes military success to Black soldiers; p15_s04 names them as agents who 'helped mankind on to this great consummation' — the strongest recognition of Black agency in the corpus. `black_soldiers_absent` does NOT apply here.
document_summary.annotator_notes: Register is semi_public_letter: Lincoln wrote this knowing Conkling would read it aloud to a large rally in Springfield. It combines epistolary informality with rhetorical polish — closer to formal_public_address than to private correspondence. p14_s02 ('The Father of Waters again goes unvexed to the sea') — celebrated rhetorical image; evaluated for cluster annotation. Source domain: the Mississippi River personified as a patriarch now free to move. Does not map to any of the six clusters (the nation-as-river is not cluster_01, and the 'Father' is not cluster_05's founding-fathers frame). Not annotated; noted for completeness. p15_s04: 'there will be some black men who can remember that, with silent tongue, and clenched teeth, and steady eye, and well-poised bayonnet, they have helped mankind on to this great consummation' — 'this great consummation' was evaluated for cluster_03 (completion of the democratic experiment). Confidence 0.68, below threshold. Not annotated. The recognition of Black soldiers as having helped 'mankind' reach a 'great consummation' is the most explicit agency-attribution to Black soldiers in the corpus.

sentences.text: Executive Mansion,
Washington, August 26, 1863.
Hon. James C. Conkling
My Dear Sir.
Your letter inviting me to attend a mass-meeting of unconditional Union-men, to be held at the Capitol of Illinois, on the 3d day of September, has been received.
It would be very agreeable to me, to thus meet my old friends, at my own home; but I can not, just now, be absent from here, so long as a visit there, would require.

The meeting is to be of all those who maintain unconditional devotion to the Union; and I am sure my old political friends will thank me for tendering, as I do, the nation's gratitude to those other noble men, whom no partizan malice, or partizan hope, can make false to the nation's life.

metaphor_instances.source_domain: biological life — the nation as a living entity that can be sustained or destroyed
metaphor_instances.target_domain: the existence and integrity of the American nation

mapping.the nation's life: the nation as a living organism whose life can be forfeit to partisan betrayal
mapping.make false to: betray — to be false to a living entity's vital interest

entailments: The nation has a life that can be sustained or lost
Political loyalty is framed as fidelity to a living being, not merely an institution
Partisan malice threatens the nation's life as disease threatens an organism

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: Those who are loyal to the Union are keeping the nation alive; those who let partisan interest override loyalty threaten its life. The frame makes Union-loyalty a biological imperative.

sentences.text: There are those who are dissatisfied with me.
To such I would say: You desire peace; and you blame me that we do not have it.
But how can we attain it?
There are but three conceivable ways.
First, to suppress the rebellion by force of arms.
This I am trying to do.
Are you for it?
If you are, so far we are agreed.
If you are not for it, a second way is to give up the Union.
I am against this.
Are you for it?
If you are, you should say so plainly.
If you are not for _force_, nor yet for _dissolution,_ there only remains some imaginable _compromise_.
I do not believe any compromise, embracing the maintenance of the Union, is now possible.
All I learn, leads to a directly opposite belief.
The strength of the rebellion, is its military--its army.
That army dominates all the country, and all the people, within its range.

Any offer of terms made by any man or men within that range, in opposition to that army, is simply nothing for the present; because such man or men, have no power whatever to enforce their side of a compromise, if one were made with them.
To illustrate.

Suppose refugees from the South, and peace men of the North, get together in convention, and frame and proclaim a compromise embracing a restoration of the Union; in what way can that compromise be used to keep Lee's army out of Pennsylvania?
Meade's army can keep Lee's army out of Pennsylvania; and I think, can ultimately drive it out of existence.
But no paper compromise, to which the controllers of Lee's army are not agreed, can at all affect that army.
In an effort at such compromise we should waste time, which the enemy would improve to our disadvantage; and that would be all.
A compromise, to be effective, must be made either with those who control the rebel army, or with the people first liberated from the domination of that army, by the success of our own army.
Now allow me to assure you, that no word or intimation, from that rebel army, or from any of the men controlling it, in relation to any peace compromise, has ever come to my knowledge or belief.
All charges and insinuations to the contrary, are deceptive and groundless.
And I promise you, that if any such proposition shall hereafter come, it shall not be rejected, and kept a secret from you.
I freely acknowledge myself the servant of the people, according to the bond of service--the United States Constitution; and that, as such, I am responsible to them.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: the bond of service--the United States Constitution
metaphor_instances.source_domain: a sworn bond of service — a covenant obligation between servant and master
metaphor_instances.target_domain: Lincoln's constitutional duty to the people — the Constitution as the binding covenant of his service

mapping.bond of service: a covenant that binds servant to duty — the Constitution as the charter of obligation
mapping.the United States Constitution: explicitly identified as the source and substance of the bond
mapping.servant of the people: Lincoln cast as the servant, the people as the master of the covenant

entailments: Lincoln's power is delegated and bounded by a covenant, not inherent
The Constitution is not just a legal document but a bond — a relational covenant
Responsibility to the people flows from the bond, not from political convenience

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: Lincoln frames his presidency as service under a covenant bond. The war's prosecution is not a policy choice but a covenanted obligation — he is bound by the bond of service (Constitution) to defend the people. This is the most explicit cluster_02 statement in the corpus: the Constitution is named as 'the bond of service.'
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: lincoln_non_agent applies: Lincoln presents himself as bound servant, not autonomous agent. His actions follow from the bond, not from personal will. This is cluster_02's primary psychological function — the war becomes an obligation of the covenant rather than a choice.

sentences.text: But to be plain, you are dissatisfied with me about the negro.
Quite likely there is a difference of opinion between you and myself upon that subject.
I certainly wish that all men could be free, while I suppose you do not.
Yet I have neither adopted, nor proposed any measure, which is not consistent with even your view, provided you are for the Union.
I suggested compensated emancipation; to which you replied you wished not to be taxed to buy negroes.
But I had not asked you to be taxed to buy negroes, except in such way, as to save you from greater taxation to save the Union exclusively by other means.
You dislike the emancipation proclamation; and, perhaps, would have it retracted.
You say it is unconstitutional--I think differently.
I think the constitution invests its Commander-in-chief, with the law of war, in time of war.
The most that can be said, if so much, is, that slaves are property.
Is there--has there ever been--any question that by the law of war, property, both of enemies and friends, may be taken when needed?
And is it not needed whenever taking it, helps us, or hurts the enemy?
Armies, the world over, destroy enemies' property when they can not use it; and even destroy their own to keep it from the enemy.
Civilized belligerents do all in their power to help themselves, or hurt the enemy, except a few things regarded as barbarous or cruel.
Among the exceptions are the massacre of vanquished foes, and non-combatants, male and female.
But the proclamation, as law, either is valid, or is not valid.
If it is not valid, it needs no retraction.
If it is valid, it can not be retracted, any more than the dead can be brought to life.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: the dead can be brought to life
metaphor_instances.source_domain: biological death and resurrection — the impossibility of reversing biological death
metaphor_instances.target_domain: the irreversibility of the Proclamation's legal validity — a valid law, like a living being, cannot be un-made

mapping.the dead: a once-valid legal act that has been revoked
mapping.brought to life: restoring the revoked act's validity — impossible as resurrection
mapping.the proclamation's validity: treated as a life state — it is either alive (valid) or dead (invalid)

entailments: Legal validity is like biological life — it can be lost but not restored by reversal
The Proclamation's effects are as permanent as biological death
Retraction of a valid law is as unnatural as resurrection

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: The organic frame applied to the Proclamation's legal status makes emancipation irreversible by the logic of natural law — what lives cannot un-live, what dies cannot un-die. The war's consequences are as permanent as biological fact.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: Ambiguity flag: the target domain is the legal validity of the Proclamation, not the nation as organism directly. This is cluster_01's source domain (life/death) applied to a legal object rather than the national body. Confidence 0.70 — below the 0.75 preferred threshold but retained because the organic metaphor explicitly structures a key legal argument: retraction is as impossible as resurrection. The body metaphor is doing real logical work here.

sentences.text: Some of you profess to think its retraction would operate favorably for the Union.
Why better _after_ the retraction, than _before_ the issue?

There was more than a year and a half of trial to suppress the rebellion before the proclamation issued, the last one hundred days of which passed under an explicit notice that it was coming, unless averted by those in revolt, returning to their allegiance.
The war has certainly progressed as favorably for us, since the issue of proclamation as before.

I know, as fully as one can know the opinions of others, that some of the commanders of our armies in the field who have given us our most important successes believe the emancipation policy and the use of the colored troops constitute the heaviest blow yet dealt to the Rebellion, and that at least one of these important successes could not have been achieved when it was but for the aid of black soldiers.
Among the commanders holding these views are some who have never had any affinity with what is called abolitionism or with the Republican party policies but who held them purely as military opinions.

I submit these opinions as being entitled to some weight against the objections often urged that emancipation and arming the blacks are unwise as military measures and were not adopted as such in good faith.
You say you will not fight to free negroes.
Some of them seem willing to fight for you; but, no matter.
Fight you, then exclusively to save the Union.
I issued the proclamation on purpose to aid you in saving the Union.
Whenever you shall have conquered all resistence to the Union, if I shall urge you to continue fighting, it will be an apt time, then, for you to declare you will not fight to free negroes.
I thought that in your struggle for the Union, to whatever extent the negroes should cease helping the enemy, to that extent it weakened the enemy in his resistence to you.
Do you think differently?
I thought that whatever negroes can be got to do as soldiers, leaves just so much less for white soldiers to do, in saving the Union.
Does it appear otherwise to you?
But negroes, like other people, act upon motives.
Why should they do any thing for us, if we will do nothing for them?
If they stake their lives for us, they must be prompted by the strongest motive--even the promise of freedom.
And the promise being made, must be kept.
The signs look better.
The Father of Waters again goes unvexed to the sea.
Thanks to the great Northwest for it.
Nor yet wholly to them.
Three hundred miles up, they met New England, Empire, Key-stone, and Jersey, hewing their way right and left.
The Sunny South too, in more colors than one, also lent a hand.
On the spot, their part of the history was jotted down in black and white.
The job was a great national one; and let none be banned who bore an honorable part in it.
And while those who have cleared the great river may well be proud, even that is not all.
It is hard to say that anything has been more bravely, and well done, than at Antietam, Murfreesboro, Gettysburg, and on many fields of lesser note.
Nor must Uncle Sam's web-feet be forgotten.
At all the watery margins they have been present.
Not only on the deep sea, the broad bay, and the rapid river, but also up the narrow muddy bayou, and wherever the ground was a little damp, they have been, and made their tracks.
Thanks to all.
For the great republic--for the principle it lives by, and keeps alive--for man's vast future--thanks to all.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: the principle it lives by, and keeps alive
metaphor_instances.source_domain: an organism that sustains itself by a vital principle and in turn sustains that principle
metaphor_instances.target_domain: the republic as a living entity whose existence depends on and perpetuates a foundational principle (democratic self-governance)

mapping.lives by: the republic draws its existence from the principle — as an organism lives by its vital function
mapping.keeps alive: the republic in turn sustains the principle — mutual dependence between organism and its animating force
mapping.man's vast future: compressed cluster_03: the republic's organic success has global and temporal implications

entailments: The republic and its founding principle are mutually sustaining — one cannot survive without the other
The republic's death would kill the principle; the principle's death would kill the republic
The war's outcome determines whether this vital mutual dependence continues

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: The republic lives by a principle and keeps it alive — if the republic dies, the principle dies; the war is necessary to prevent the organism's death and thereby preserve the principle for 'man's vast future.' The global-historic stakes (man's vast future) are entailed by the organism frame.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: 'Man's vast future' (cluster_03 resonance, confidence 0.72) — the republic-as-experiment frame compressed into three words. Not separately annotated; noted here.

sentences.text: Peace does not appear so distant as it did.
I hope it will come soon, and come to stay; and so come as to be worth the keeping in all future time.
It will then have been proved that, among free men, there can be no successful appeal from the ballot to the bullet; and that they who take such appeal are sure to lose their case, and pay the cost.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: will then have been proved that, among free men, there can be no successful appeal from the ballot to the bullet
metaphor_instances.source_domain: logical/empirical proof — a proposition demonstrated by evidence
metaphor_instances.target_domain: the war as the proof of the proposition that democratic self-governance cannot be overturned by force

mapping.proved: the war functions as empirical demonstration — not just military victory but logical proof
mapping.no successful appeal from the ballot to: the proposition: democratic decision cannot be overturned by armed force
mapping.free men: the experimental subjects — the proposition is about what is possible 'among free men'

entailments: The war is not just a military conflict but a scientific experiment with a definite answer
Proof requires the war's Union victory — losing would disprove the proposition
The result will be permanent knowledge: 'it will then have been proved' — epistemic finality

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: The war's Union victory is necessary not just to preserve the nation but to prove a proposition about democratic governance. Losing is not just military defeat but logical refutation. This makes victory an epistemic obligation, not merely a political preference.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: The ballot-to-bullet phrase is Lincoln's most compressed statement of the democratic-experiment proposition: democracies claim that the ballot is the final arbiter; when that claim is challenged by armed force, the war is the test of whether the claim is true. The proof-frame makes the war's outcome epistemically necessary.

sentences.text: And then, there will be some black men who can remember that, with silent tongue, and clenched teeth, and steady eye, and well-poised bayonnet, they have helped mankind on to this great consummation; while, I fear, there will be some white ones, unable to forget that, with malignant heart, and deceitful speech, they strove to hinder it.
Still, let us not be over-sanguine of a speedy final triumph.
Let us be quite sober.
Let us diligently apply the means, never doubting that a just God, in his own good time, will give us the rightful result.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: a just God, in his own good time, will give us the rightful result
metaphor_instances.source_domain: a just judge and timekeeper who dispenses outcomes according to righteousness and divine schedule
metaphor_instances.target_domain: Providence as the ultimate arbiter of the war's outcome — God will deliver the just result when the time is right

mapping.a just God: God as righteous judge — justice is built into the divine nature
mapping.in his own good time: God manages historical timing — the result comes when divinely appointed, not when humanly desired
mapping.will give us the rightful result: Providence dispenses outcomes — the Union's cause is righteous and will receive its rightful verdict

entailments: The Union cause is righteous — God's justice aligns with it
Human impatience is inappropriate — divine timing supersedes human urgency
The outcome is guaranteed by divine justice, not just military effort

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: The war must be fought because a just God will give the rightful result — abandoning the effort would be refusing the divinely-promised outcome. Providence is not passive here (as in the Springfield Farewell) but active: God will give the result. The obligation to persist comes from the certainty of divine justice.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: lincoln_non_agent: Lincoln and the Union are instruments applying the means; God gives the result. The agency structure: humans apply means → God gives outcome. Compare with Second Inaugural (doc_021) where God is not just a giver but a punisher. Here the cluster_06 frame is active-benevolent rather than punitive.

sentences.text: Yours very truly
A. Lincoln
